scenes.narrative: The guests have fled. The townhouse is silent save for the ticking of a mantel clock and a low, persistent hum from the séance room. Lady Ashworth sits in the hallway, her composure finally broken. Madame Vesper is in the séance room, kneeling beside the table, whispering. Lord Pemberton is in the study, pouring himself a very large brandy. The manifestation has faded, but the air still carries that prickling charge — the sense that something is present, watching, waiting. You have pieces of the truth. Now you must decide how to end this.

choices.text: Confront Lady Ashworth with what you know — the fraud, the debts, the conspiracy.
Help Vesper complete the binding — seal whatever was summoned before it grows stronger.
Drag Pemberton back to the séance room. He built the mechanism — he can help dismantle it.

scenes.narrative: The binding fails. The sigils on the table flare with cold grey light, and the temperature plummets. The presence that was summoned during the séance manifests fully — not a ghost, not a spirit, but something older and less comprehensible. It fills the room like smoke, pressing against your mind with a weight that has nothing to do with the physical world. Vesper screams. The gas lamps explode. In the sudden darkness, you must face what has been called.

choices.text: Recite the Grey Dawn dismissal ward from Vesper's journal.
Hold your ground. Refuse to yield to the presence's psychic assault.
Flee the séance room. Some battles cannot be won.
Command the presence to depart — your will against its hunger.
Retreat. The presence is too strong.

scenes.narrative: The truth comes out in the cold light of the hallway. Lady Ashworth and Lord Pemberton conspired to fake the séance — she to save her estate, he to protect his gambling debts. But Madame Vesper, hired as a theatrical prop, turned out to be the genuine article. She used real Grey Dawn protocols, and something answered. The fraud and the supernatural collided, and a woman is dead — one of the guests, found in the garden, her heart stopped by terror or by something worse. You lay out the evidence for Inspector Graves when he arrives. Ashworth weeps. Pemberton is led away in handcuffs. Vesper sits in silence, staring at her hands. The Rationalists' Circle will have questions. The Grey Dawn will have more.

onEnter.target: Hermetic Order of the Grey Dawn

scenes.narrative: The dismissal ward takes hold. The Grey Dawn sigils flare once — brilliant, blinding white — and the presence recoils. The temperature rises. The pressure lifts. Vesper slumps against the table, exhausted but alive. The binding circle beneath the table cracks down its centre, spent. Whatever was summoned has been sent back, though the seal is imperfect — it may not hold forever. In the aftermath, you help Vesper to her feet. 'Thank you,' she says. 'I was a fool to attempt this without a full circle.' Lady Ashworth's fraud is a secondary matter now. The Grey Dawn will want to know what happened here. So will you.

onEnter.target: Hermetic Order of the Grey Dawn

scenes.narrative: The evening ends in uneasy compromise. The presence fades on its own — or perhaps it simply withdraws, sated or bored. The guests are gone. The townhouse is cold and silent. Lady Ashworth's fraud remains unexposed, though you carry the knowledge of it. Pemberton's debts remain unpaid. Vesper disappears into the London night, her journal clutched to her chest. No one is arrested. No one is saved. The séance room door is locked, and Lady Ashworth asks you, very politely, to leave. The case is closed, but nothing is resolved. The presence may return. The fraud may continue. And you are left with the uncomfortable certainty that in London, the rational and the supernatural are not opposites — they are collaborators.
